image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |

This has a quite of a big impact due to the fact that the white race originated the sports around the world. It shows that a lot of people from different countries can also compete as an athlete in any sports.

It shows that in the professional athletic field they are  more players and coaches of color than any other race. 
The catchy title I would give to this specific graph is Diversity In The Professional Athletic Field.

raw_comment:
What I noticed: I noticed that baseball has the lowest percentage of colored players.  The players have higher rates of colored players than that of the coaches, and only baseball has less than 50% of their payers identify as colored.  The Women's National Basketball Association and the National Basketball Association both have the same percentage of colored players, and similar percentages of colored coaches.

What I wonder:  Why are the coaches so much less diverse than the players?  What may have caused this? Does the time at which the data was recorded affect the amount of diversity?

Impact: This graph has the potential to impact our community both positively and negatively.  Although it does promote racial equality in sports by encouraging the fact that people of color are able to be treated equally, it also shows that there is a lack of diversity among the coaches, which could potentially spread the message that people of color are less worthy of leading than those not of color.

Heading: Are Sports Teams Really as Diverse as We Think?

Is it because of the basketball influence for people of color, or other reasons? I also wonder why football has such a low percentage of coaches of color at 13% despite having the second largest percentage of players of color, at 74%.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
I notice that many of the players are people of color, almost always over 50%, all the way up to 83%, except in Major League Baseball. But, not many coaches are people of color, always under 50% and as low as 13% in football.

What do you wonder?
I wonder how a white coach is going to be able to relate to the players when they encounter things like racism, especially in current times. I also wonder why there is such a disparity between representation of people of color in coaching positions and players. Is it because of racism and discrimination?

What impact does this have on you and your community?
This gives kids watching sports on tv the idea that people of color can’t be coaches and can only be players. This also means that there are fewer role models of people of color in coaching positions for little kids who want to do that when they grow up. This causes the kids to be discouraged and even fewer coaches will be people of color.

What’s going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
Are Sports Teams Really That Diverse?

raw_comment:
I noticed that there is a higher percentage of colored players than colored coaches on any one of these major sports. However, baseball has the lowest percentage of colored individuals on the team. 

I wonder why people of color are not hired to higher positions in the sports industry. This could be because they are coincidentally not qualified enough or just because of racism. 

This graph tells me that people of color from my generation can play sports without feeling left out or underrepresented. The issue here is that the coaches' data is not showing as much diversity as the player data. This could be discouraging to individuals of color- showing that it's not possible for them to accomplish more than just playing the sport. 

My headline for this graph would be "Is Diversity Balanced Between Coaches and Players?"

- I notice that the majority of players (at least 60%) are people of color in all sports leagues except for Baseball. Another observation I made was that a much smaller percentage (maximum of 41%) of head coaches are people of color.

- This impacts our community because the major sports leagues fail to have a diverse set of head coaches. The data indicates that people of color have a disadvantage in becoming a head coach compared to non-colored people. 
- A catchy headline for this graph would be "Racial Diversity in Sports: What are the Percentages of Players and Coaches of Color in Major Sports Leagues?"

raw_comment:
What I noticed: I noticed that all the sports listed have more players of color compared to the head coaches of color. The head coaches of color percentages for all the sports range between 13%-41%, not even reaching 50%. To add, baseball is the only sport where colored players aren’t the majority. 

What I wonder: I wonder why the colored baseball players aren’t the majority when colored players in football, basketball, and soccer are the majority. What may have caused this? could it be because of how the sport is presented to the public?

Impact on me and my community: Our community is impacted both positively and negatively by this graph. It impacts us positively because the diversity among the players can encourage other sports fans to play and shows that in some sports, colored people have the opportunity to be treated equally. It also impacts us negatively because the lack of diversity among the head coaches shows us how people of color might not have the opportunity to lead and could possibly reinforce the stereotypical job types present in society.

My title:  “Colored individuals aren’t getting equal opportunities on both sides of the major sports leagues, study says.”

What impact does this have on you and your community?  This affects those of color who aspire to be coaches of these major sports leagues. A possible explanation is racial prejudice. As coaches are in a state of greater power and responsibility over a team, which represents their community. This, unfortunately, gives those of color an extremely hard journey to become a coach compared to other races.  This should not be the case and this graph clearly points this fact out. This is a probable reason for the disparity of race.

This has a negative impact on the greater community. because while people of color will see other people of color succeeding in sports, they will not see similar idols they can look up to in the head coaching department. raw_comment:
I noticed that the majority of players in all sports, except for major league baseball, are players of color. Basketball in particular is dominated by people of color with an approximate 4:1 ratio for both genders, while baseball is almost equal but leaning towards white people with a 3:2 ratio. 
In every poll, it is clear that the percentage of head coaches of color are significantly lower than the percentage of players of color. In National League Football, most of the players are of color (74%), whilst head coaches of color are only 13%. I wonder why is there such a noticeable difference between players of color and coaches of color? In the sports Major League Baseball and Major League Soccer, the percentage of head coaches of color are 20% lower than players of color. This is a smaller difference compared to the 60% difference in National League Football. As for Women's and Men's National Basketball Association, the percentage difference between coaches of color and players of color is around 50%. 
Although some differences are more noticeable than others, we can clearly see that people of color are less likely to be head coach in a major sports league. This has an impact on the community around us because it shows people of color have different opportunities to be in power than white people.
An interesting headline would be, "Player of Colors vs. Head Coaches of Color in different major sports leagues".

raw_comment:
I noticed that the majority of players in all sports, except for major league baseball, are players of color. Basketball in particular is dominated by people of color with an approximate 4:1 ratio for both genders, while baseball is almost equal but leaning towards white people with a 3:2 ratio. 
In every poll, it is clear that the percentage of head coaches of color are significantly lower than the percentage of players of color. In National League Football, most of the players are of color (74%), whilst head coaches of color are only 13%. I wonder why is there such a noticeable difference between players of color and coaches of color? In the sports Major League Baseball and Major League Soccer, the percentage of head coaches of color are 20% lower than players of color. This is a smaller difference compared to the 60% difference in National League Football. As for Women's and Men's National Basketball Association, the percentage difference between coaches of color and players of color is around 50%. 
Although some differences are more noticeable than others, we can clearly see that people of color are less likely to be head coach in a major sports league. This has an impact on the community around us because it shows people of color have different opportunities to be in power than white people.
An interesting headline would be, "Player of Colors vs. Head Coaches of Color in different major sports leagues".

raw_comment:
I notice that MLB has the least amount of players of color (40%), and the second least amount of managers/head coaches who are people of color (20%). MLS has the highest amount of head coaches of color (41%), while the NBA and WNBA have the highest amount (tied at 83%) of players who are people of color. Over 70% of NFL players are people of color, but only 13% of the coaches are.

I wonder why MLB has so few coaches of color -- 2 in 5 players are people of color, but only 1 in 5 coaches are people of color. I wonder why 13% of NFL coaches are people of color, but 74% -- almost 3 in 4! -- of players are people of color.

If people don't see themselves in their idols -- like if a person of color wants to grow up and become a NFL coach, but they never see a person of color be an NFL coach -- it's harder for them to believe they could become ____[whatever they want to be]____. If someone wants to be a MLB player, but they only see certain players, who don't look like them, they'll probably feel discouraged and might not go after their dream.

Percentage of People of Color in Professional Sports.

raw_comment: I noticed that both of the Basketball Associations have high percentages which means that people of color would have a better chance playing or coaching basketball. The NFL has also done a pretty good job at keeping their players diverse, however not as much for their coaches. The MLS has done a pretty decent job at keeping their players diverse at 60% as well as having the highest percentage for head coaches of color at 40%. On the other hand, the MLB has less than 50% of their players of color so they haven't done as well of a job at keeping their players diverse. I wonder why the MLS has a lower percentage than the NFL, WNBA, and the NBA for their players, yet have a higher percentage for the coaches. It's odd that their players/coaches ratio of color is somewhat proportionate but then the MLS has a different ratio than the rest. This doesn't really have an impact on me unless I wanted to play professionally in the WNBA, however I'm more interested in soccer (NWSL) which is the women's version of MLS who's data isn't in this data set. On the other hand, this probably impacts the community in a way that they would be more concerned for the MLB seeing that their percentages are lower than 50%. In the graph, the data shows the diversity of players and coaches within different sports leagues. As for the title/main idea of the data, I would name it "Modern Professional Sports Leagues Diversities".

raw_comment:
I notice that MLB has the least amount of players of color (40%), and the second least amount of managers/head coaches who are people of color (20%). MLS has the highest amount of head coaches of color (41%), while the NBA and WNBA have the highest amount (tied at 83%) of players who are people of color. Over 70% of NFL players are people of color, but only 13% of the coaches are.

I wonder why MLB has so few coaches of color -- 2 in 5 players are people of color, but only 1 in 5 coaches are people of color. I wonder why 13% of NFL coaches are people of color, but 74% -- almost 3 in 4! -- of players are people of color.

If people don't see themselves in their idols -- like if a person of color wants to grow up and become a NFL coach, but they never see a person of color be an NFL coach -- it's harder for them to believe they could become ____[whatever they want to be]____. If someone wants to be a MLB player, but they only see certain players, who don't look like them, they'll probably feel discouraged and might not go after their dream.

Percentage of People of Color in Professional Sports.

1. What do you notice?
 
I notice that in sports like basketball and football, African-American players dominate the sport. Soccer and baseball however, are much closer to the 50% mark. Also, I noticed that none of the five sports had over 50% of minority head coaches/managers. 
 
2. What do you wonder?
 
I wonder, will there ever come a time where one of those sports eclipses the 50% of minority head coaches/managers mark?
 
3. What impact does this have on you and your community?
 
I am somebody who eats, breathes, and sleeps sports. I’m also someone who is passionate about racial issues and equality. I think it is absolutely disgusting how minorities are and have been treated in this country. These graphs just show another example of racial inequality.
 
4. What’s going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
 
Sports Need A Color-Blindness Test

These graphs show the diversity within today's US sports leagues. I notice that the data displays that in all the sports shown except baseball, the players of color make up more than half of each sports population. I also notice that among the coaches, there are no leagues which coaches of color makeup at least half of the coaching population. The smallest percentage being that of the NFL at 13%, and the greatest being the MLS at 41%.

The two basketball leagues (women's & men's ) have very similar diversity numbers, yet all of the other leagues seem to have no other correlation between them. I wonder what changes the data between leagues? History or roots of the sport? 

I live in a predominantly white state and having played many sports, I know that there aren't many players of color within my state. These graphs impact me because they help me better understand the diversity within sports across the US. Rather than just in New Hampshire.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
I notice that these graphs separately display the percentages of players of color and coaches of color across different sports leagues of the nation. Along with this, the athletes of color seem to be well represented, in some circumstances even being the majority, however, in all of the leagues there seems to be an inequality for white coaches compared to coaches of color.

I wonder how these graphs have shifted over time since the beginning of integration in sports. Obviously, at one point there was a time that people of color weren’t even allowed in the American leagues. I wonder if once the ban was lifted, the levels of diversity in athletes slowly increased or if there was a certain point where they skyrocketed.
 
This does not affect me directly, however it affects the people around me and in my community as it shows the representation of people of color in sports across America. It shows that athletes are extremely diverse, and in some cases, the majority of athletes are people of color, however, regarding coaches, there is not as much of a representation. This shows some employment opportunities could still have segregation involved, and there’s room to improve going forward.

This graph depicts the level of diversity in both athletes and coaches across 5 sports leagues of America.
 
Catchy Headline: The Diversity and Disparity of American Sports

raw_comment:
What do you notice? The percentage of players of color in major sports leagues is very high. 83% of the Women's National Basketball Association's and National Basketball Association's players are people of color while 74% of players in the National Football League are people of color. This is significantly high compare to other career fields. The Head coaches percentage, however, is totally different. The majority of percentage of head coaches of color range from 13% to 30%, very low considering how people of color are everyone who is not white. The percentage of Major League Soccer is both surprising and reasonable for me. The percentage is definitely more balance when it comes to players and head coaches. This probably can be explained by the large number of countries playing soccer in the world

What do you wonder? Why the percentage of head coaches of color is significantly less than players of color.

What impact does this have on you and your community? Looking at the percentage of head coaches who are people of color, I can see how much work needed to be done. There should be more opportunities being provided to the people of color to coach and to lead in major sports leagues

raw_comment:
In all four of the graphs, I notice that the majority of all the players in every league are playing of color. Every league is above 60% players of color to be exact. However, I also noticed that in the managerial spots for each league, there was not a single league with more than 50% coaches/managers of color.
I wonder why teams and leagues are not focusing on racial diversity at the coaching positions? (However, I feel that in the coach’s role one shouldn’t argue someone’s skin color rather how their team does with he/her as a coach.)
I wonder why it varies so drastically per league with people of color in the managerial roles?
I wonder if the diversity of people of color in the managerial role has anything to do with the sport? 
This unfortunately has little impact on my community because very few of my family friends and friends in general focus on sports and more specifically the color of the coaches/players’ skin. I feel that there needs to be more awareness in smaller towns etc. However, it is hard because we all live in a predominately white state so there is never really forced racism or on the other hand diversity on sports teams. When I do watch sports I am not focusing on who is white vs who is a player of color I am simply focusing on if my team is winning or losing. 
New Headline - “Racial inequality is at the top of many of our sports organizations.”

raw_comment:
Based on the graphs shown at the top of the article, I noticed right away that there is a much lower percentage of diversity among the coaches of these major sports leagues when compared to the actual diversity among the players. A potential issue with this could be that coaches often maintain their jobs for a very long time when compared to the players. 
I wonder why this study excluded important sports leagues such as the National Basketball Association and the National Hockey League. While the NBA has a very high percentage of diversity amongst the players in their league, it also wields a very low diversity percentage for their coaches. Then, when considering the NHL, there are only 43 professional players in the entire league that are of color. Out of upwards of 620 players, it truly shows how undiverse a sport like that is. So a sport like that would be a good equalized contradiction to the graphs being shown.
This has a large impact on me and my community because I come from a city with very little diversity. Growing playing sports, most of my teams have always been predominantly white and the same for our coaches. Meaning that because of the lack of diversity within our community, also reflects itself upon the sports we play.
The graphs in this article depict the gap between the diversity of the players of sports and the diversity of the coaches of the sports in large national sports. My alternative title would be: “Diversity on the field, similarity on the sideline”

## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
I notice that the visual shows the percentages of POC players as well as POC coaches in different professional sports. The only sport listed which has majority white players is Major League Baseball. The rest of the sports listed have majority POC players. Every sport listed has a fairly low number of POC coaches. The majority of coaches seem to be white. I wonder if racism embedded into the sports industry prevents people of minoritized ethnicities from becoming coaches. I also wonder why such a large percentage of baseball players are white. Do former sports players become coaches? If so, why are the racial demographics of coaches so different from the racial demographics of players?

This impacts my community because my city has several sports teams (a basketball team, a baseball team, and a soccer team). My city also has a majority Black population. It is very possible that African American coaches in my city are discriminated against and have trouble finding jobs coaching major teams.

raw_comment:
I notice how in men's and women's basketball, they seem to have the highest amount of diversity in players at around 83%, and a fair amount of coaches at around 1/3 and 1/4. I notice how National football has a high amount of players of color at around 74%, but the lowest amount of coaches of color at 13%. Lastly, Baseball seems to be low in both areas, lacking players of color and coaches below the 50% mark.

I wonder what these graphs would look like in maybe 10 years' time. Generally, we would expect all of these numbers to keep increasing, as a more diverse community is crucial to advancing as a whole. I also wonder what is the current status of other predominantly white sports such as golf, or tennis.

My community is definitely affected since New Hampshire is one of the whitest states in the country. My community is definitely trying to push for more diversity in sports, and really in anything we do. It's important to get people from all walks of life involved, to better lift our community upwards.

raw_comment:
I notice that overall, there seems to be more players of color than white players, but more white coaches than coaches of color. I also notice that for both mens and womens basketball, the percentage of players of color is the highest of all five sports leagues, while baseball has the least number of players of color by about 43%. I wonder why there are so many more players of color compared to non colored players, but the opposite for coaches. I feel like the ratios should be closer to each other, because then it would keep the same percentage of colored people in these five sports leagues.
	The effect this has on my community is that these graphs show how much of an outlier we are. From my experience playing sports my whole life, I have noticed that there are a lot more white players than players of color, and the same for coaches. This fact must arise because of how many white people, and people of color live in our community. If there were more people of color living in our community, we would see more players of color. But since our community is heavily white, the number of white players outnumbers that of the colored players.
	I would title the name of these graphs, “Racial Diversity in Professional Sports”

I wonder what history has to do with this.  Do certain sports have a lower or higher player/coach percentage of color because of its history, or is there no relationship.  In addition to this, why does MLS have the best ratio of player to coach?

## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
I noticed that for all five of the sports, there is a much higher percentage of players of color, than coaches of color. Male and female basketball has the highest percentage of players of color at 83%, but the percentage of coaches of color is still fairly low. The sport with the highest percent of colored coaches is Major League Soccer at 41%, which means that 59% of the rest of the coaches are white.
 
I wonder if these percentages would be significantly different five or ten years ago, or in five or ten years ahead. I also wonder why the National Football League has such a high number of players of color, but the numbers for the coaches of color are so low. 
 
This impacts my community and I for many reasons. Since Portsmouth lacks diversity, we don’t necessarily see as much racism as much as it exists around the country. As displayed in the graphs, there is clear evidence that there aren’t many professional sport coaches of color, as there are players. This tells us that there is some aspect of racism, and there needs to be changes regarding this. 

“The Lack of Diversity in Professional Sports”

raw_comment:
I notice that there are more players of color in major sports leagues than there are head coaches of color. The Women’s National Basketball Association and the National Basketball Association have the highest percentage of players of color at 83 percent. Major League Baseball has the lowest percentage of players of color at only 40 percent. The National Football League has the lowest percentage of head coaches of color, 13 percent, and Major League Soccer has the highest percentage of coaches of color at 41 percent. 
I wonder why there is a higher percentage of players of color than coaches of color in the five major league sports? Does this reflect the number of players on each sports team or the number of coaching positions? Why does the National Football League have such a high percentage of players of color but such a low coaches of color percentage?
These graphs demonstrate to me and my community that systemic racism and obstacles exist for people of color. It seems that more barriers prevent people of color from becoming coaching staff instead of players. I believe this reflects a systemic preference for non-people of color to obtain leadership positions, which needs to be abolished. 
"Leadership Diversity Eludes Major League Sports"

raw_comment:
After examining each graph, I noticed that four of the five sports listed have done a great job of diversifying their players. However, all are rather lacking in the diversification of those in leadership positions. I also noticed that in sports like Basketball, a sport dominated by people of color, the number of head coaches of color is rather low. The percentage of head coaches of color is even beaten out by soccer, which has 23% less players of color. I wonder why sports like football and basketball, which are dominated by people of color, have so few people of color in leadership positions. I also wonder why baseball, when compared to the other sports given, has such a low number of players of color. This impacts myself and my community as I live in New Hampshire, one of the whitest states in the country. I would imagine that if you were to explore these statistics for highschool or college sports in New Hampshire that the results would be even more profound. While it is clear from the graph that people of color now have a well deserved place in the player base, it also shows that work can be done amongst coaches. Therefore, this graph provides possible insight into what these numbers could look like within New Hampshire.

Possible Headline-“Leadership positions fall behind in diversity”

raw_comment:
I notice that for about every three players of color, there’s only one person of color who is a head coach or manager. I wonder whether each team has a similar proportion of players of color or if some have close to all and some have close to none. (Although a large majority of players are people of color either way). This impacts me and my community because of the biases and expectations that come with these kinds of patterns. In the example of sports teams and coaches/managers, there are many more white people in power even though people of color are the reason they are successful. This may keep people of color from those leadership positions in the future if change isn’t actively sought. 

A possible headline: People of Color are the Driving Force of Major League Sports Teams… But Who’s in Charge?: Racial Disparity in Sports

raw_comment:
Notice: I definitely notice a strong absence of head coaches that are POC. Unsurprising really that there is almost always going to be at least double the number of POC players than head coaches.

Wonder: I wonder how big their sample size is and what the numbers really are. I also wonder what each association plans on doing to change this because it really should be more correlated. 

This may not have a great impact on my immediate community, but I am from Iowa so a couple of our college teams are kinda good I guess. It mainly has an impact on the players of color who I feel for because they are more than likely to not see a coach who is also POC during their career (throughout college athletics and throughout their career if they choose that path). 

Possible Headline: Broken Promises: National Athletics Associations Refuse To Level With Their Commitments to Racial Equity.

raw_comment: I notice the proportion of head coaches of color is significantly lower than the proportion of players of color in the specified major sports leagues. Less than 50% of every league’s coaching staff are people of color. However, more than 50% of every league’s players are people color, besides baseball. MLS has the highest percentage of coaches of color, but is not necessarily the most diverse because it has the second-lowest percentage of players of color. This led me to believe there's not a direct correlation between the diversity in players and diversity in leadership. White Americans are the racial majority of the U.S., therefore, statistically, there are fewer people of color. However, I wonder why there's such a significant disparity between the percentage of players of color and coaches of color in every major sports league? I also wonder, as more awareness is brought to movements like BLM, will leadership diversity increase in years following? I think it's especially important to bring light on these racial injustices to communities like my own. Being a state that's 94% white, the system's racial inequities don't negatively impact the majority of my community. This data validates what people of color in my community and in the entire country, deal with daily. This graph compares the percentage of players of color to head coaches of color in major U.S. sports leagues, showing the discrepancy. So, “Is the Playing Field Really Level for Those Seeking Sports Leadership?”

raw_comment: From this graph, I noticed that quite a high percentage of players across several sports are players of color, whereas not many coaches are. This may mean that talent leads to POC landing player roles, but because of systemic racism and other things in society it is harder for POC to climb up in managerial roles and earn higher wages. Also, there is quite a gap in the percentages between players of color and coaches of color. The largest gap is in the NFL with a 61% gap, and the second-largest gap is in the WNBA with a 58% gap. From this graph, I wonder how the data in this graph from the years 2019 and 2020 compares to past years. It would also be interesting to see how this data compares with sports teams across the globe. One last thing I wonder is what our society can do to break down whatever challenges or barriers are preventing more POC from becoming head coaches. I feel that this data impacts my community in the fact that it is reflective of it. Much of our society is built on systemic racism and for this reason, POC face challenges every day because of their skin color. The fact that such a glaring difference in racial distribution is apparent in big sports leagues makes me wonder how much worse it is on a smaller, more local scale. The alternate title I have created for this graph is "Lack of Diversity in Sports: How Equal Really is the Field?"

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
1. What do you notice?
I noticed that more than 50% of the players in each major sports league, except for Major League Baseball, are people of color. I also noticed that the percentage of coaches of color for each major sports league was much lower than 50%.
 
2. What do you wonder?
I wonder why there are so few coaches of color in each major sports league. Is there something preventing more coaches of color from earning these positions? I also wonder why less than 50% of Major League Baseball is composed of players of color. I wonder how all of these percentages would compare to data from 20 years ago.
 
3. What impact does this have on you and your community?
This impacts me and my community because people of color in my community who aspire to coach these leagues may not have the opportunity to, due to their race. To me, this is appalling because someone should never have a lesser opportunity because of their race. This has obviously been an ongoing issue in our world that needs to come to an end. 
 
4. What’s going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
This graph clearly shows that there are many less coaches than players of color in these major sports leagues. The graph depicts the lack of coaches of color in these leagues.
Headline: “Racism In The Game.”

raw_comment:
1) There is a much higher overall percentage of players of color then there are of head coaches of color. And these variables don’t seem to be directly correlated with one another. Major League Soccer has the second lowest percentage of players of color (60%) but also has the highest percentage of lead coaches of color (41%). So “most diverse league” is a hard title to give. But “least diverse league” is pretty easy. With the lowest percentage of players of color (40%) and the second lowest percentage of lead coaches of color (20%), the MLB is, in my opinion, the least diverse league. That being said, even in the MLB, there is a clear gap between the percentage of players of color and the percentage of lead coaches of color.
2) I wonder why Major League Soccer is one of the least diverse for players but the most diverse for head coaches.
3) I come from one of the least diverse states in the country. When there is racial bias, it is easy for many in my community to ignore it because it doesn’t affect them personally. I have seen this happen as a person of color, and it's frustrating. I think it is important for people, especially in less diverse communities, to be aware of racial injustice.
4) The main idea of this display is to show that there is a large gap between the percentage of players of color and the percentage of lead coaches of color. One potential headline is “When it comes to team leadership, are we telling people of color to sit on the sidelines?”

raw_comment:
“The Never Ending Game Against Racism, but is the Game Even Fair?” 

I noticed that there is a higher percentage of players of color than head coaches of color. This speaks volumes in that it is less so lack of opportunity but lack of ability for POC to move up in the industries that our society lives off of. The fact of the matter is that there is so much more that goes into the earnings of leadership roles, like coaching as opposed to roles such as players, that stretches beyond talent and ability and starts to fall into the unwarranted areas of politics and duplicity.  

I wonder why the number of colored players almost always outnumbers the colored coaches by 3 times. I wonder why this is even an occurring issue. How could we eliminate the obstacles present in the sports industry that people of color face that enable them from achieving the same positions as caucasians?

The impact this issue has on my community is ever so present and relevant, and has been for the last 400 years. Although I live in a predominately white community, this issue is still present. If this is occurring on such page platforms such as the professional sport industry, it is indubitably present in smaller proportions.

The graph clearly displays the discrepancy between the percentage of lead coaches of color and the percentage of players who are of color in the major league sports industry. It clearly shows that the amount of colored players is much higher than the amount of coaches of color.

raw_comment:
I noticed that the NFL has the largest percent difference between head coaches of color and players of color (61%). The MLS has the smallest percent difference (19%). The NFL only has 13% of coaches of color. On the other hand, the WBNA and NBA have 83% of coaches as people of color. Although the MLB only has a 20% gap between coaches of color and players of color, they only have 20% of coaches as minorities and 40% of players as minorities. The percent of colored players is the least out of all the leagues. I also noticed that the data for the WBNA was taken from 2019 whereas the rest of was taken in 2020. 

I wonder how these percentages compare to say a decade ago. Did the percent difference between coaches of color and players of color grow or shrink? I also wonder how they would compare a decade into the future. Would the numbers drastically change or stay relatively the same? I'm also curious how diversity graphs would look internationally (i.e. European and Asian sports). Is this graph indicative of how people of color are treated in professional sports? 

This impacts my community because while diversity has grown very much in sports, discrimination still takes place. While I think discrimination has lessened for players, I think it still poses challenges and obstacles for staff and personnel of color. Even though the US has made progress, there's still a long way to go. 

A headline could be, "Diversity in Professional Sports: Staff vs. Players".

raw_comment: I notice that there are more players of color in basketball and football than players of color in baseball and soccer. I also notice that there are more head coaches of color in the Major League of Soccer despite having the lowest percentage of players of color. I wonder as to why it is that we see these trends among race with specific sports. I wonder if it is a cultural aspect that makes it so that more people of color are inclined to play basketball and football over soccer because there are way less people of color who actually play soccer. I wonder if they feel unwelcomed or whether it is a predominantly white sport. The impact that this has on my community is that it helps me understand a lot more as to why I see this trend. Coming from a predominantly person of color community, I do understand that a lot more people are interested in football and basketball because it is a sport that many of us were exposed to growing up from a young age. Possible headline: "Is Sport Leadership Aligned with the Players' Race?"

raw_comment:
I notice that in the most recent year (only one given) for each of these major sports leagues, those with the largest disparity between the percentage of players of color and leadership of color are the NFL (61% gap), followed by the WNBA (58% gap) and then the NBA (50% gap). It’s interesting that the leagues with the smallest gaps (MLB & MLS) have fewer players of color also.

I wonder how these percentages compare to those from previous years. What are these initiatives referenced? Have the leagues shown progress? How are the players within these leagues affected by these gaps and are their concerns being addressed?

As a high school teacher, I think of how data like this affects my students. While those within the POC community could be encouraged by the percentage of players of color within the leagues, what difficult thoughts/questions come when they see the lack of representation in the leadership percentages. When seeing this data, would my students be motivated to dig deeper, asking themselves “Are others concerned about this?” or “What is being done about it?” My hope is that, despite their initial reaction, they would find their voice to ask the questions troubling them and their power to research and advocate further!

Despite progress or initiatives that have been implemented to address the discrepancies seen above, more work needs to be done. Catchy headline? “Commissioners: Mind the Gap!”

## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
1. What do you notice?
 
I notice that the proportion of players of color is much higher than the proportion of head coaches of color in every major league sport depicted in the graph. I also notice that players of color make up the majority of all players in every major league sport except for baseball. Conversely, head coaches of color make up less than half of all head coaches in every major league sport. 
 
2. What do you wonder?
 
I wonder why this pattern emerges. Why do people of color make up large proportions of major league sports teams, but not large proportions of coaching staff? Are there any specific roadblocks in place that prevent people of color from assuming coaching positions in major league sports?
 
3. What impact does this have on you and your community?
 
	This affects my community because if racial biases are ingrained in major league sports industries, it may be difficult for people of color in my community to become professional coaches if that is something they aspire to do. 
 
4. What’s going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
 
This graph shows the discrepancy between the proportion of players of color and the proportion of head coaches of color in major league sports. 
Headline: “The end of the rainbow: Are people of color being prevented from becoming head coaches in major league sports?”

raw_comment:
I notice that these segmented bar graphs show a significant disparity between the percentage of players of color and head coaches of color in major sports leagues. This is most apparent in the NFL, where 74% of players are POC while only 13% of coaches are—a difference of 61%. The gap is apparent in all five sports shown, which indicates that the phenomenon is not likely to be due to random chance. 
I wonder why the gap in percentages varies across different types of sports. For example, there is only a 20% gap in baseball compared to the 61% gap in football. I also wonder why there is such a large discrepancy between the percentage of players and head coaches of color in the first place. 
This graph makes me reflect on how we seem to be reluctant to have people of color in leadership positions, and how we can do better in the future. In my community, I am reminded that it is essential to uplift and empower people of color to give them equal leadership opportunities. 
This graph shows a clear pattern: the percentage of lead coaches of color is significantly lower than players of color across major sports. A headline could be “Diversity in Team Leadership: Are We Truly Equal?”

raw_comment:
Thanks to all students who responded.  We had so many great catchy headlines that capture the main ideas of the graphs:  “Sports:  Diversity or Lies on Head Coaches of Color? The Color Spectrum in Sports” by Rosa of Los Angeles, “Behind the screen. Is it really diverse in the front office?” by Liam, Chen, and Alex and “The Colour of Sports” by Madison and Kai, both of Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, “The Roles People of Color Hold in Sports Leagues” by Lauren of Academy of Saint Elizabeth in Morristown, New Jersey, and “The Divide in Diversity: The Gap Between Diverse Athletes and Coaches” by OBBI of Eden Prairie High School, Minnesota.
 
You can continue to post responses.  Please check back by Friday, Feb. 5 to see the Reveal with a link to the original New York Times article, some questions about the map, and a few Stat Nuggets.
 
Please visit our archive to see all graphs in this series (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphs).
View our archives that link to all past releases, organized by topic (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTopicIndex), 
graph type (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTypeIndex) and 
Stat Nugget (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsStatNuggetsIndex). 
 
Signing off – Sharon Hessney of Boston, who thinks progress in diversifying professional sports has been too little too slowly, and Corey Andreasen, grade 5 mathematics teacher at the American School of the Hague, the Netherlands, who thinks the patters we see in these graphs is an indication of a much wider problem.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |

raw_comment:
Part 1

What do you notice? I immediately noticed the same percentage of people of color that are in the NBA and WNBA. However, the NBA has a greater percentage of colored head coaches than the WNBA by five percent. I believe the number of coaches of color and coaches who are women collaborates; the fact is that both of their numbers are extremely low compared to those of white males. The reasoning for this? I am not sure. Possibly because men who are white are more likely to get the job because of their appearance, even if the other colored coaches are a better fit for the job. My greatest assumption of why white coaches are more common in these major league sports than those of color is because these issues of racial inequality have not been addressed as profoundly as they have been this past year, especially with the Black Lives Matter Movement. Many of these coaches have been doing their jobs for what could be the last decade. I do think it would be unfair for a perfectly capable coach to be fired if he or she is white because the team thought they needed more diversity within their coaching staff. I do not think race should be involved in the decision of hiring coaches; whoever best fits deserves the position.

Because of this, I wonder why the number of head coaches of color are substantially lower than number of players. Is this lower number because of race or is it just that way because? This impacts my community because if coaches are mostly white because of race, that will seriously damage our country. Every person in America deserves a chance to pursue whatever career or dream they wish without race playing a role. 
New Headline: "Does Race Play a Role In Sports Teams?"

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
CATCHY TITLE// Racial Diversity: Sideline Statistics 

looking at these graphs I notice that there are higher percentages of players of color than there are coaches of color for every sport listed. I know that coaches stay in the league longer than players do and that there are more players than coaches. it just makes me wonder why POC don't get the representation that they deserve in higher positions, not just in sports but in any white-dominated industry. The impact that this could have on people in my community is devastating. I myself have watched sports my whole life and I used to dream of being a basketball coach in the WNBA. I never thought that I couldn't reach my goal because I always saw people who looked just like me doing it. Now I have come to realize that my fellow teammates, classmates, and friends had doubts as children because they never saw people who looked like them doing what they dreamed of, so they thought that they weren't allowed or that they didn't want people who looked like them to be in those positions. I can only imagine what it would feel like to have a dream but believe you couldn't reach it because of the skin color that you were born with. I think that these sats are a prime example of the argument that there should be more representation and equality in sports and positions of high regard.

raw_comment:
What do I notice? I notice that there are many more white coaches than black coaches. There is a problem with these graphs though. I can see they're trying to say that racism is still around, but this is actually pretty proportional to America. About 70% of America is made of people not of color. There's obviously going to be more non-people of color. It's silly to not think so.

I wonder why the NHL was excluded from this. The NHL has very few black players at the moment, around 43, since 2020. A typical NHL roster consists of 20 players, which is about 620 players. And only 43 of them are black. There are currently NO black head coaches in the NHL. You can argue racism on this, which is partially true,

Recently there have been more and more NHL coaches being fired from their position due to racist accusations, and them turning out to be true. I'd like to see the NHL in here, so people could question, why does the NHL have so few POC players?

raw_comment:
I immediately notice the disparity between players and coach percentages. The darker pink bars represent the players of color, and the lighter pink bars represent the head coaches of color. I also notice that in the graph they included the women's basketball association and the national basketball association. With these two graphs compared right next to each other, I notice that the women's basketball association has 5% fewer head coaches of color than the national basketball association. I  wonder why that is? I wonder why the national football league has the fewest amount of head coaches of color? I also wonder why the head coaches of the color statistic are less than 50% for all of the sports? This impacts my community as it provides knowledge and statistics to help inform people of the different percentages of diversity in different sports in regards to players and head coaches.

raw_comment: I noticed that there are many more players of color than coaches of color. The length of time that a player can play is much shorter than a coaching time. Players age out of the sport, but coaches can remain in a coaching position for many years.  I wonder what the average time a coach stays with their team. This plays a big impact on our community because it shows how much our country has changed. It's weird to think that years ago people of color were separated from just using the same bathroom as white people. And now there is a big mix of different ethnicities on our national sports teams. One of the greatest baseball players, Hank Aaron, was not allowed to play with a white team until 1953. He still faced discrimination after that. This shows how much our country has grown and changed. I also wonder how hard it is to get on one of the competitive teams with your race. Many sports teams want to create diversity, so they want different races on their team. This also reminds me of colleges, many colleges look to enroll people of a different race than what the average is at that certain college.

raw_comment:
As I look at this graph, I notice that in every major league sport, the percentage of players of color is higher than the percentage of head coaches of color. I can’t help but wonder if this is because of the smaller sample size of the head coaches.

In the MLB’s case, there are only 30 managers total, one for each team. Each team has, at most, a 40-player expanded roster. With 30 teams in the league, there can be a maximum of 1,200 players that play for the MLB at once. The difference between the minuscule amount of managers and the much larger number of players makes me wonder how much the sample size comes into play. If each team had a 1:1 manager/player ratio, would the percentage of head coaches of color still only be 20 percent?

The issue of the small sample size also is relevant in each one of the other sports. The NFL has the highest number of head coaches, at 32. The NFL has more than 1,500 active players every year, which gives a much higher opportunity for diversity than the mere 32 coaches. When it comes down to it, I think the reason the percentages of diversity are much lower in coaches is because of availability. The average NFL player’s career only lasts 3.3 seasons, whereas the average NFL head coach’s career is nearly a full year longer, averaging out at 4.3 seasons. To me, the long career, as well as the limited availability, are very large factors in whether or not a position is diverse or not, because the positions are not being filled as quickly.

raw_comment:
What I noticed: I noticed that there is much more diversity in the player role then there is in the coaching role. Many of the sports have at least two times the percent of players of color then coaches of color, the one exception being the M.L.S. One other thing I noticed is that there are far less colored players in the M.L.B. 

What I wonder: I wonder why companies or sports corporations have not been giving out more jobs to colored people in the coaching position. With many of the players in sports now being colored I think a colored coach may end up being able to work with them better. I also wonder why there is such a lack of diversity in the M.L.B. Something just seems off when you look at all the other sports diversity compared to the M.L.B’s. 

Impact: This statistics show the truth about professional careers in sports being both a player and a coach. It makes me think that it must be a lot harder to be good at basketball if you are white then if you are colored. Seeing these statistics may call others to try and do something about the lack of diversity in coaching or leadership roles. I thought many sports organizations have said that they try to add diversity but this shows that they are not doing so in any leadership roles.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
In every type of sports league depicted in the graph, there is a larger percentage of players who are people of color than there are head coaches who are people of color. Additionally, in most leagues except Major League Baseball, the percentage of players of color exceeds the percentage of head coaches of color by a large degree. 

What do you wonder?
I wonder why there is such a large gap between players of color and head coaches of color? Why is the player of color:coach of color ratio so large? 

What impact does this have on you and your community?
Throughout my schooling years, I have met two people of color who hoped to become coaches of popular sports teams. Will these peers of mine have trouble finding jobs in this industry because of the gap that occurs between players and head coaches of color? Discrimination still exists in our world whether it is purposeful or not. It continues to affect the people of color in my community and my country. The information in this graphic might be an example of this.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
Group 6: Rose Zwettler, Kate Shepard, Grace Urban, and Alex Scott
“The Coaching Gap: Underrepresentation in Professional Sports”

We noticed that there are a lot more players of color in each group than head coaches of color. This made us wonder why this gap in player and coaching representation exists. We also noticed that soccer and baseball have the lowest percentage of colored players. On the other side of it, Major League Soccer has the highest percentage of head coaches of color. Another thing we noticed was that all of the data is from 2020 except for one group, the Women’s National Basketball Association which is from 2019. This makes you curious as to why they would include data from a different year only for one of the groups. This also made us wonder why they would only show one women's sport out of the 5 sports showcased. Lastly, we noticed that for the MLB, managers were counted as head coaches instead of the actual coaches being counted. This made us wonder why they would have to count managers and not head coaches like they did for every other sport. From our observations and wonderings, we thought of the data’s impact on our community. For one, it makes it clear that to our community that there is a major problem with underrepresented coaches of color in professional sports. From that it makes us question what we as a community can do to lessen this issue.

raw_comment:
“Diversity seen in sports” 

I noticed that there is a higher percentage of players of color than head coaches of color. That major league soccer has the most head coaches of color and that national football league has the least. I also noticed that the women's national basketball association has the most players of color while the major league baseball has the least.

I wonder why the number of colored players almost always outnumbers the colored coaches by 3 times. I wonder why soccer players have so many more coaches of color compared to every single other sport. I wonder why the NFL a sports league with a very high percentage of colored players has the lowest percentage of colored coaches, especially compared to the MLB with such a low percentage of colored players but a high percentage of colored coaches. 

When I look at these statistics, It makes me wonder if people of color are getting the same opportunities in the community when it comes to different roles in sports. It looks like they get great opportunities to play the sports but not as many opportunities to be managers or coaches in the sports. We should be looking at giving people of color more opportunities to be in management positions at a younger age, so that when they grow up we will have more of them managing sports teams professionally.

raw_comment:
Summary:
 
It is a graph showing the percentage of players and coaches with color in some sports. Based on the sports and the names of the sports associations, we can assume that these are sports associations that are based in the United States.  We’ve noticed that most of the sports have a vast majority of colored players, while most of the coaches are white. Colored athletes in basketball are well represented compared to other sports. However, coaching has a much lower percentage of minorities. We wonder why the head coaches are less likely to be people of color because for each sport the percentage of color for coaches is lower than players? We also wonder why soccer has such a high percentage of coaches of color compared to the other sports, could it be because it is more widely played in other parts of the world so there’s a wider variety of coaches? This data may impact our community because it is discouraging coaches of color to pursue a coaching position on more diverse teams. However, this could influence minorities or people of color to join sports, where everybody is included. You can become a professional player of any sport no matter your skin color. 
 
Our catchy headline for this graph is “The divide in diversity: the gap between diverse athletes and coaches”

raw_comment: Based on the data provided in the graph, we found that despite a majority of sports having large percentages of players of color, there are far less coaches of color to represent them. As an example, the National Football League consists of 74% players of color, however, has only 13% coaches of color serving in the league. We also noticed that Major League Baseball tends to have the fewest players of color in comparison to other sports featured in the graph. These findings led us to question, why do the percentages of players of color seemingly have little correlation to the amount of coaches of color? Furthermore, the information presented in the graph affects both ourselves and our community in several ways. Underrepresentation in sports can have a major effect on young people of color who hope to pursue a career in professional sports and potentially hurt their aspirations. Our group also believed it was important for players and coaches to be able to have people around them who are different and have experienced different things in order for them to grow and be able to create relationships. Our headline for this article would be : “Major league sports in relation to coaches and players of color."

raw_comment:
Diversity in Pro Sports
This graph shows the number of players that are people of color on various sports teams based on the percentage of players on the team, while also showing the number of people of color that are in charge of coaching these teams. As a group, we noticed that people of color make up a very high percentage of these sports teams, despite making up a very small amount of the coaches and managers. Our group is curious about a few things in this graph. For one; which group makes up the gray portion of each bar? We also want to know why there is a noticeable lack of players of color within the MLB. The data collection process also makes us wonder, what specific teams were involved, who gathered the data? Though arguably most importantly, we wonder about the paycheck difference between coaches and players. We want to know what are some consequences of having so few people of color acting as coaches.

## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
raw_comment:
We notice that the national basketball associations have the highest percentages of players of color of all the sports. After the basketball associations, the national football league has the second highest percentage of players of color. I also notice that the head coaches of color don't even come close to the percentage of players of color. Soccer has the highest percentage of coaches of color which makes sense since it is a more worldly sport. The more “American” sports tend to have lower diversity in coaching staff. These sports would be football and baseball. We wonder why there are less coaches of color overall compared to players of color. Is it because there are simply more players of color or is it due to discrimination amongst coaches in those specific sports/leagues. Also, what could be the criteria/standards for being a coach? Maybe there’s a specific reason why there are more players of color rather than pursuing a head coach career. This graph impacts our community by highlighting the lack of coaches and even people of color in leadership positions throughout school sports. This puts emphasis on the issue of racial inequality in the sports system. We think that if there were more coaches of color there would be more kids of color that would feel comfortable participating in these activities and that kids of color would also get more opportunities in the sport they chose to participate in.

raw_comment:
I notice that compared to players of color, there are far less head coaches of color. Especially in football where 74% of the NFL is made up of player of color and only 13% of the NFL head coaches are people of color.

I wonder what percent of the staff on these major sports leagues such as assistant managers, coaches, nutritionist, etc. are people of color?

This impacts my community because for awhile now we've seen people of color excel at sports. However, it is just as important people of color have the opportunity to be coaching staff and be involved in more ways than just being players. If teams utilize people of color to play for their teams and win games, they should have just as much respect for people of color when it comes to hiring coaching staff.

Diversity in major sports leagues off the field.

Welcome to Wednesday's moderation of “What’s Going On in This Graph?”  

Today’s graphs compare five professional sports leagues – football, basketball – women’s and men’s, soccer and baseball, regarding 
the percentage of players and of head coaches who are people of color.

From 9 am – 2 pm E.T. today, share with us your responses online to these three questions -- "What do you notice?",  "What do you wonder?", and “What impact does this topic have on you and your community?”

Plus, please share with us a catchy headline that captures the ideas in these graphs. 

By Friday morning, we’ll reveal the article that included these graphs and much more.  We look forward to reading your discoveries.

raw_comment:
I notice that the data all comes from 2020 except for the WNBA, which used 2019. This data could have been used since the percentage of player of color (83%) matched the NBA percentage of 2020. I also notice how the MLB's players of color (40%) equal less than half of the NBA or WNBA (83%). 

I wonder why the data of the WNBA was not available for the 2020 season. I also wonder why overall there are much less head coaches of color per team than players of color. Could this be because there are just more players, or are head coaches discriminated against in these leagues.

This data may impact my community by discouraging people of color in pursuing a coaching career due to its lack of diversity. On the other hand, this data may encourage people of color to continue working at their sport (besides MLB which is 40% players of color) in order to make a career.

Diversity in Professional Teams

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |

This shows me that coaching staffs are often not as diverse as the teams themselves. With less diverse coaching staffs, there are not as many people of color in coaching positions to look up to.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
- **Small-multiple horizontal bar chart** (two bars per league showing percentages).

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Small multiples / faceting by league**: one panel per sports league arranged in a row.
- Within each panel, **two separate (not stacked) horizontal bars** for two groups.

## 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Sports league (NFL, WNBA, NBA, MLB, MLS) | Categorical/nominal | **Faceting** into separate panels; also shown as **panel title text** |
| Year (e.g., 2020, 2019) | Temporal | **Text label** within each league panel |
| Group (Players of color vs Head coaches of color) | Categorical/nominal | **Color/hue** (two shades) and **row position** (top vs bottom bar) within each panel; referenced in legend |
| Percentage (share who are “of color”) | Quantitative | **Bar length** (horizontal position/extent); **numeric labels** (e.g., 74%, 13%) on/near bars |
